sourceMovie.title: Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion for the same reason it broke through in 1997: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a science fiction rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Kazuya Tsurumaki keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The animation and science fiction blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Evangelion: 1.0 You Are (Not) Alone
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion clicked for you, Evangelion: 1.0 You Are (Not) Alone (2007) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and science fiction, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Evangelion: 2.0 You Can (Not) Advance

faq.question: What should I watch after Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion?
faq.answer: Try Evangelion: 1.0 You Are (Not) Alone, Evangelion: 2.0 You Can (Not) Advance, Ghost in the Shell 2: Innocence—each shares DNA with Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion?
faq.answer: Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion (1997) is best described as Animation, Science Fiction, Drama, Fantasy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Neon Genesis Evangelion Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
